title: Plant diversity and community structure in Bukhansan National Park, Korea
authors: Kim Jae-won, Lee Soo-young, Park Min-ji
institutions: Seoul National University, Korea National Arboretum
abstract: We surveyed vascular plant communities along elevational gradients in Bukhansan National Park. A total of 412 species were recorded, with significant changes in species richness across elevation bands. Disturbance by visitor trampling was identified as a major driver of community composition near trails.

title: Visitor Behavior and Trail Erosion in Bukhansan National Park
abstract: Trail erosion in Bukhansan National Park is closely associated with visitor density and off-trail hiking. GPS tracking of 430 visitors revealed that 23% deviated from designated paths. Targeted infrastructure improvements are proposed to reduce soil compaction and erosion rates.

title: Wildlife corridor connectivity in the Seoul metropolitan green belt: Bukhansan case study
authors: Hong Sung-jae, Yoon Ji-hyun, Kim Byeong-su
abstract: Urban fragmentation severely restricts mammal movement in the Seoul green belt. Connectivity analysis using least-cost path modeling shows that Bukhansan is functionally isolated from Dobongsan for medium-sized mammals. We recommend two bridge corridors to restore landscape permeability.

title: Landslide susceptibility mapping in Bukhansan using machine learning
institutions: Korea Institute of Geoscience and Mineral Resources
journal: Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences
abstract: Random forest and logistic regression models were applied to map landslide susceptibility across Bukhansan National Park. The random forest model achieved AUC of 0.89. Slope, aspect and land cover emerged as the most important predictive variables. Results can inform trail closure decisions during heavy rainfall events.

title: Remote sensing-based vegetation monitoring of Bukhansan National Park (2000–2020)
authors: Shin Hye-jin, Park Jong-min, Lee Eun-ji
abstract: Twenty years of NDVI time series derived from Landsat and Sentinel-2 imagery reveal a 6.3% increase in forest greenness in Bukhansan National Park. Phenological shifts consistent with climate warming were detected across all elevation zones. Invasive species expansion was documented in the southeastern slopes.

title: Historical landscape changes around Bukhansanseong Fortress: A paleoecological perspective
abstract: Pollen analysis from sediment cores near Bukhansanseong Fortress reveals forest clearance events correlated with Joseon-dynasty military activities in the 17th century. Recovery of oak-dominated woodland by the 19th century is attributed to reduced human pressure following dynastic policies.

title: Air quality inside vs. outside Bukhansan National Park: Particulate matter and ozone analysis
abstract: PM2.5 concentrations measured at six stations within Bukhansan were consistently 18–32% lower than urban reference sites in northern Seoul. Ozone showed the opposite pattern with peak afternoon values 14% higher in forested areas. These data quantify the air quality provisioning ecosystem service of the park.

title: Bat community composition and echolocation call analysis in Bukhansan National Park
abstract: Acoustic monitoring over two seasons recorded 11 bat species in Bukhansan National Park. Pipistrellus abramus was most abundant, accounting for 41% of passes. Cave roost surveys confirmed breeding colonies of Rhinolophus ferrumequinum at three sites. Light pollution along park boundaries was negatively correlated with bat activity.

title: Socioeconomic valuation of Bukhansan National Park recreation services using travel cost method
authors: Lim Da-eun, Park Hyun-woo, Cho Ji-su
abstract: Using survey data from 1,200 park visitors, we estimated the annual recreational value of Bukhansan National Park at KRW 487 billion using the individual travel cost method. Day-trip users showed the highest consumer surplus. Results support continued investment in trail maintenance and visitor facilities.

title: Bryophyte diversity and microhabitat preferences along streams in Bukhansan
abstract: A total of 184 bryophyte species including 27 liverworts were recorded from 42 riparian survey plots in Bukhansan National Park. Species richness peaked at mid-elevation streams with stable humidity and moderate light availability. Three red-list mosses were newly confirmed from the park.
